problem_addressed: Theoretical understanding of estimation bias in Expected Calibration Error (ECE) when using binning strategies is limited.
core_claim: The paper provides the first comprehensive analysis of ECE estimation bias for uniform mass and uniform width binning, establishing improved upper bounds, revealing optimal bin numbers, and extending bias analysis to generalization error via information theory.
key_contributions: Comprehensive analysis of estimation bias for uniform mass and uniform width binning strategies
Derivation of improved upper bounds on bias with enhanced convergence rates
Identification of optimal number of bins to minimize estimation bias
Extension of bias analysis to generalization error using information-theoretic approach
Demonstration of nonvacuous bounds via deep learning experiments
novelty: First theoretical analysis of ECE estimation bias for common binning strategies, novel connection to generalization error via information theory, and derivation of optimal bin count.
method_summary: Establishes upper bounds on ECE estimation bias for uniform mass and uniform width binning, derives optimal bin sizes, and extends analysis to generalization error using information-theoretic bounds to evaluate ECE on unknown data.
method_components: Bias analysis framework for ECE estimation
Mathematical derivation of optimal bin count
Numerical validation with deep learning models
key_innovation: Connecting ECE bias analysis to generalization error via information theory to enable practical evaluation of calibration on unknown data.
assumptions: Use of test and training datasets for ECE estimation
Standard binning strategies (uniform mass and uniform width)
theoretical_grounding: Formal upper bounds on bias with improved convergence rates, mathematical derivation of optimal bin count, and information-theoretic generalization bounds.
title: Information-theoretic Generalization Analysis for Expected Calibration Error
